```{r}
  filter(year == 2022, geocode %in% c(id_mojui, id_santarem)) %>%
  summarise(
    pop_mojui      = population[geocode == id_mojui],
    pop_sant       = population[geocode == id_santarem],
    gdp_mojui      = pib_brl[geocode == id_mojui],
    gdp_sant       = pib_brl[geocode == id_santarem],
    gdp_mojui_2024 = pib_brl_2024[geocode == id_mojui],
    gdp_sant_2024  = pib_brl_2024[geocode == id_santarem]
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    pop_share_mojui      = pop_mojui / (pop_mojui + pop_sant),
    gdp_share_mojui      = gdp_mojui / (gdp_mojui + gdp_sant),
    gdp_share_mojui_2024 = gdp_mojui_2024 / (gdp_mojui_2024 + gdp_sant_2024)
  )

data_pib_adjusted <- data_pib_per_capita_panel %>%
  left_join(shares_2022, by = character()) %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  # Step A: backcast Mojuí from Santarém's original values
  mutate(
    pib_brl = case_when(
      geocode == id_mojui & year < 2013 ~
        pib_brl[geocode == id_santarem] * gdp_share_mojui,
      TRUE ~ pib_brl
    ),
    pib_brl_2024 = case_when(
      geocode == id_mojui & year < 2013 ~
        pib_brl_2024[geocode == id_santarem] * gdp_share_mojui_2024,
      TRUE ~ pib_brl_2024
    ),
    population = case_when(
      geocode == id_mojui & year < 2013 ~
        population[geocode == id_santarem] * pop_share_mojui,
      TRUE ~ population
    )
  ) %>%
  # Step B: subtract Mojuí's imputed share from Santarém's original values
  mutate(
    pib_brl = case_when(
      geocode == id_santarem & year < 2013 ~
        pib_brl * (1 - gdp_share_mojui),
      TRUE ~ pib_brl
    ),
    pib_brl_2024 = case_when(
      geocode == id_santarem & year < 2013 ~
        pib_brl_2024 * (1 - gdp_share_mojui_2024),
      TRUE ~ pib_brl_2024
    ),
    population = case_when(
      geocode == id_santarem & year < 2013 ~
        population * (1 - pop_share_mojui),
      TRUE ~ population
    )
  ) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(year <= 2022)
```

#### 3.2.5. Construct GDP per capita and log transformation

I compute GDP per capita as the ratio of real GDP (in 2024 Reais) to municipal population, and apply a natural logarithm transformation to compress the distribution and reduce the influence of extreme values, which is standard practice for income variables in panel analyses:

$$
GDPpc^{2024}_{it} = \frac{GDP^{2024}_{it}}{Population_{it}}
$$

$$
\ln GDPpc^{2024}_{it} = \ln\left(\frac{GDP^{2024}_{it}}{Population_{it}}\right)
$$

where $GDP^{2024}_{it}$ is real municipal GDP in 2024 Reais and $Population_{it}$ is the interpolated population of municipality $i$ in year $t$.

```{r}
data_gdp_per_capita_panel <- data_pib_adjusted %>%
  mutate(
    gdp_per_capita_2024     = if_else(
      !is.na(population) & population > 0,
      pib_brl_2024 / population,
      NA_real_
    ),
    log_gdp_per_capita_2024 = log(gdp_per_capita_2024)
  ) %>%
  select(geocode, municipality, year,
         pib_brl_2024, population,
         gdp_per_capita_2024, log_gdp_per_capita_2024)
```

#### 3.2.6. Check and validate the data

I verify that the Mojuí dos Campos and Santarém series are internally consistent by confirming that the two municipalities sum to the original Santarém aggregate in all pre-2013 years, for both nominal and deflated GDP and for population. I also inspect the dataset for missing values, confirm that all geocodes are valid 7-digit IBGE identifiers, and cross-validate a random sample of observations against IBGE's online *Sistema IBGE de Recuperação Automática* (SIDRA) platform.

```{r}
# Santarém + Mojuí should recover the original combined total in all pre-2013 years
data_pib_adjusted %>%
  filter(geocode %in% c(id_mojui, id_santarem), year < 2013) %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    combined_pib      = sum(pib_brl),
    combined_pib_2024 = sum(pib_brl_2024),
    combined_pop      = sum(population)
  ) %>%
  left_join(
    data_pib_per_capita_panel %>%
      filter(geocode == id_santarem, year < 2013) %>%
      select(year,
             original_pib      = pib_brl,
             original_pib_2024 = pib_brl_2024,
             original_pop      = population),
    by = "year"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    check_pib      = round(combined_pib      - original_pib,      2),
    check_pib_2024 = round(combined_pib_2024 - original_pib_2024, 2),
    check_pop      = round(combined_pop      - original_pop,       2)
  ) %>%
  select(year, check_pib, check_pib_2024, check_pop) %>%
  print()
# All three check columns should be 0 (or negligible floating-point error)
```

### 3.3. Electoral competitiveness

#### 3.3.1. Understand the data

The electoral data used in this study comes from the **Tribunal Superior Eleitoral (TSE)**, Brazil's supreme electoral authority, which publishes official results for all federal, state, and municipal elections. The raw microdata was accessed through the **Base dos Dados** initiative [@basedosdados], a Brazilian open-data infrastructure project that standardizes, documents, and makes publicly available a wide range of administrative and governmental datasets. Base dos Dados pre-processes the TSE raw files to harmonize variable names, resolve encoding inconsistencies, and assign stable municipality identifiers compatible with the IBGE geocode standard.

I use the municipal election results for mayoral races (*cargo = "prefeito"*) from the **ordinary elections** (*eleição ordinária*) held in 2000, 2004, 2008, 2012, 2016, and 2020. The raw dataset is structured at the voting section level (*seção eleitoral*), meaning each row records the number of votes received by a given candidate in a given voting section of a given municipality. The key variables are: `ano` (election year), `id_municipio` (7-digit IBGE municipality code), `id_candidato_bd` (unique candidate identifier assigned by Base dos Dados), `sigla_partido` (party abbreviation), `turno` (round), `tipo_eleicao` (election type), and `votos` (votes cast).

#### 3.3.2. Clean and transform the data

The construction of the electoral competitiveness variables follows three sequential aggregation steps.

**Step 1 — Filter and pre-process.** I restrict the data to first-round results (`turno == 1`) of ordinary elections (`tipo_eleicao == "eleicao ordinaria"`), which drops supplementary elections (*eleições suplementares*) that are not comparable to regular electoral cycles. This choice follows the standard approach in the Brazilian electoral competition literature [@chamon2019; @arvate2013], which uses first-round vote shares to ensure comparability across all municipalities, given that only municipalities above 200,000 registered voters are subject to a second-round runoff rule. I also convert the `votos` variable from character to integer, as it is stored as a string in the raw TSE files.

**Step 2 — Aggregate to the candidate $\times$ municipality $\times$ year level.** Since the raw data is recorded at the voting-section level, I sum votes across all sections within each municipality for each candidate, identified by `id_candidato_bd`. This produces one row per candidate per municipality per election year, with the variable `candidate_votes` representing total votes received by that candidate in that municipality.

**Step 3 — Compute vote shares.** I calculate the total number of valid votes cast in each municipality-year as the sum of `candidate_votes` across all candidates, and then divide each candidate's total votes by that sum to obtain their individual vote share:

$$
s_{kit} = \frac{V_{kit}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N_{it}} V_{jit}}
$$

where $V_{kit}$ is the total votes received by candidate $k$ in municipality $i$ at election year $t$, and $N_{it}$ is the number of candidates running in that municipality-year.

```{r}
# Step 1: Filter and pre-process
mayors <- data_elections_raw %>%
  filter(
    cargo        == "prefeito",
    turno        == "1",
    tipo_eleicao == "eleicao ordinaria"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    year  = as.integer(ano),
    votes = as.integer(votos)
  )

# Step 2: Aggregate to candidate × municipality × year
candidates <- mayors %>%
  group_by(year, id_municipio, sigla_uf, id_candidato_bd,
           numero_candidato, sigla_partido, numero_partido) %>%
  summarise(
    candidate_votes = sum(votes, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Step 3: Compute vote shares
candidates <- candidates %>%
  group_by(year, id_municipio) %>%
  mutate(
    total_votes = sum(candidate_votes),
    vote_share  = candidate_votes / total_votes
  ) %>%
  ungroup()
```

#### 3.3.3. Construct the competitiveness variables

From the candidate-level dataset, I collapse to the municipality $\times$ year level and construct five variables, described below.

##### Effective Number of Candidates (ENC)

The **Effective Number of Candidates** ($ENC$) is the standard measure of electoral fragmentation in the political economy literature [@laakso1979; @chamon2019; @arvate2013]. It is defined as the inverse of the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) of vote-share concentration:

$$
ENC_{it} = \frac{1}{\sum_{k=1}^{N_{it}} s_{kit}^{2}}
$$

where $s_{kit}$ is the vote share of candidate $k$ in municipality $i$ at election $t$. The ENC equals 1 when a single candidate runs uncontested and increases as both the number of candidates and the evenness of vote distribution grow. It thus captures not only the raw count of competitors but also how competitively their votes are distributed, giving greater weight to races where multiple candidates hold substantial shares. An ENC of 2, for instance, indicates the electoral equivalent of two equally matched candidates, regardless of how many nominal candidates appear on the ballot.

##### Margin of Victory (MoV)

The **Margin of Victory** ($MoV$) is defined as the difference in vote shares between the first- and second-placed candidates:

$$
MoV_{it} = s_{(1)it} - s_{(2)it}
$$

where $s_{(1)it}$ and $s_{(2)it}$ are the vote shares of the winner and the runner-up in municipality $i$ at election $t$, respectively. Smaller values indicate closer races and therefore greater electoral pressure on the winner. Unlike winner vote share alone, MoV correctly classifies fragmented races where the winner holds a low absolute share but faces no single strong challenger, and closely contested two-candidate races where the winner holds a majority but barely defeated one opponent. This distinction is substantively relevant: a mayor who won with 36% of the vote in a four-candidate race may face less re-election risk than one who won with 53% against a single strong rival.

##### Competitive dummy

I construct a binary indicator equal to 1 if the election was competitive, defined as a race in which the margin of victory is below 10 percentage points:

$$
\text{Competitive}_{it} = \mathbb{1}[MoV_{it} < 0.10]
$$

This threshold is standard in the literature [@ballotpedia] and identifies elections where the outcome was genuinely uncertain. The dummy is used in robustness checks and descriptive analyses; the continuous $MoV$ is preferred in the main specifications to avoid information loss at the threshold.

##### Uncontested dummy

I flag municipalities where only one candidate ran for mayor:

$$
\text{Uncontested}_{it} = \mathbb{1}[N_{it} = 1]
$$

For uncontested races, $MoV$ is undefined (no runner-up exists) and is set to `NA`. $ENC$ is set to 1 by construction. These observations are retained in the dataset but excluded from analyses where electoral competition is the variable of interest, as they represent the structural absence of competition rather than a particular degree of it.

```{r}
data_competitiveness <- candidates %>%
  group_by(year, id_municipio, sigla_uf) %>%
  arrange(year, id_municipio, desc(vote_share)) %>%
  summarise(
    enc          = 1 / sum(vote_share^2),
    mov          = vote_share[1] - vote_share[2],
    winner_share = vote_share[1],
    n_candidates = n(),
    total_votes  = first(total_votes),
    winner_party = sigla_partido[1],
    winner_id    = id_candidato_bd[1],
    .groups      = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    competitive  = as.integer(mov < 0.10),
    uncontested  = as.integer(n_candidates == 1),
    mov          = if_else(uncontested == 1, NA_real_, mov),
    enc          = if_else(uncontested == 1, 1, enc)
  )
```

#### 3.3.4. Check and validate the data

The final dataset contains 5,113 municipality-year observations across six election cycles (2000, 2004, 2008, 2012, 2016, and 2020). I verify the absence of duplicate municipality-year records and confirm that all `id_municipio` values match valid 7-digit IBGE geocodes. I also inspect the distribution of each variable by election year to identify implausible values and confirm consistency over time.

I cross-validate a random sample of municipality-year observations against the official TSE results published on the TSE website (<https://resultados.tse.jus.br/oficial/app/index.html>), confirming that the aggregated vote totals and winner identities are consistent with the official records.

@tbl-competitiveness-summary presents descriptive statistics for the continuous competitiveness measures, by election year.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-competitiveness-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Descriptive statistics for electoral competitiveness variables, by election year"

data_competitiveness %>%
  filter(uncontested == 0) %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    N             = n(),
    `ENC (mean)`  = round(mean(enc,  na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    `ENC (sd)`    = round(sd(enc,    na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    `MoV (mean)`  = round(mean(mov,  na.rm = TRUE), 3),
    `MoV (sd)`    = round(sd(mov,    na.rm = TRUE), 3),
    `% Competitive` = round(100 * mean(competitive, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    `% Uncontested` = round(100 * mean(uncontested), 1)
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```


#### 3.3.5. Expand to a yearly panel and carry forward electoral values

The competitiveness dataset produced in the previous steps contains one observation per municipality per **election year** (2000, 2004, 2008, 2012, 2016, and 2020). Since the dissertation analysis requires a balanced municipality-year panel covering the full period from 2000 to 2020, I expand the dataset to include all intervening years and carry the electoral values forward across each four-year mayoral term.

The underlying rationale is straightforward: the competitiveness of the election that brought a mayor to office characterizes the political context under which that mayor governs for the entirety of their term. The ENC, MoV, and derived dummy variables computed for election year $t$ therefore apply to years $t+1$, $t+2$, and $t+3$, until the next election resets the values. No interpolation is performed between election cycles, as electoral competition has no meaningful continuous trajectory within a term.

Formally, for any non-election year $t'$ belonging to the term initiated by election $t$:

$$
ENC_{it'} = ENC_{it}, \quad MoV_{it'} = MoV_{it}, \qquad \forall \ t' \in \{t+1, t+2, t+3\}
$$

I first construct a complete panel shell containing all combinations of municipalities and years from 2000 to 2020, then left-join the competitiveness data onto it, and finally apply a downward fill within each municipality. An indicator variable `election_year` is added to distinguish observed election-year values from carried-forward ones.

```{r}
# Full municipality × year panel shell
panel_shell <- data_competitiveness %>%
  distinct(id_municipio, sigla_uf, geocode) %>%
  cross_join(tibble(year = 2000:2020))

# Join and carry forward
panel_competitiveness <- panel_shell %>%
  left_join(
    data_competitiveness %>%
      select(year, id_municipio, enc, mov, winner_share,
             n_candidates, total_votes, winner_party, winner_id,
             competitive, uncontested),
    by = c("year", "id_municipio")
  ) %>%
  arrange(id_municipio, year) %>%
  group_by(id_municipio) %>%
  fill(enc, mov, winner_share, n_candidates, total_votes,
       winner_party, winner_id, competitive, uncontested,
       .direction = "down") %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(
    election_year = as.integer(year %in% c(2000, 2004, 2008, 2012, 2016, 2020))
  )
```

Any remaining `NA` values after the fill reflect municipalities that are absent from the TSE records for a given election cycle — typically units created by municipal splits after 2000 that did not yet exist as independent electoral jurisdictions. These are treated as structural missings and retained as `NA` rather than imputed.
